title: geofencing — PHP attribute contracts
description: Two class-level attributes. `#[Geofenceable]` on models that carry a fence (registered against the fenceable morph map). `#[GeofenceSubjectAlias]` on models that call the evaluator on their own behalf (registered against the subject morph map). Both refuse duplicate aliases across the codebase at boot.

attributes.name: Geofenceable

params.name: alias
params.description: The snake_case identifier persisted to `geofence_checks.fenceable_type`. E.g. `branch`, `facility`, `venue`, `event`, `delivery_zone`.

attributes.runtime_behaviour: The CompileMorphAliases listener (bound to foundation::ApplicationBooted) scans classes for #[Geofenceable]. Each is registered in the fenceable morph map via Relation::enforceMorphMap(). Boot fails on duplicate aliases OR on aliases whose target class doesn't implement the Geofenceable interface.
attributes.companion_trait_note: Using the trait provides default implementations of all Geofenceable interface methods reading from the standard 7 columns. Models with non-standard geometry storage can implement the interface directly without the trait.

attributes.name: GeofenceSubjectAlias

params.name: alias
params.description: The snake_case identifier persisted to `geofence_checks.subject_type`. E.g. `staff_clockin`, `attendance`, `attendance_submission`, `reception_visit`.

attributes.runtime_behaviour: Same discovery pipeline as #[Geofenceable] but registered against the subject morph map. Boot fails on duplicate aliases. NO required interface — subjects don't have any evaluator surface; they're just polymorphic targets for the audit trail's 'what caused this evaluation' pointer.

namespace_separation: Fenceable + subject alias namespaces are separate. The string `branch` may only appear once in the fenceable map; the string `staff_clockin` may only appear once in the subject map. But `branch` in fenceable + `branch` in subject would be legal (though confusing — boot warns).
